turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion this morning in Case 23-51, Bissonnette versus LePage Bakeries, CK Sales and Flowers Foods. Flowers Foods is the second largest producer and marketer of bakery foods in the United States. It makes Wonder Bread, tortillas, bagels, Butterscotch Krimpets and Jumbo Honey Buns in more than 40 bakeries in 19 states and markets its products throughout the country. Neal Bissonnette and Tyler Wojnarowski are franchisees who own the rights to distribute Flowers products in certain parts of Connecticut. Flowers would bake the bread and buns and send them to a warehouse in Waterbury. Bissonnette and Wojnarowski would pick them up and distribute them to local grocery stores and other outlets. Now, to purchase the rights to their territories, Bissonnette and Wojnarowski signed distributor agreements with Flowers. Those contracts require that any claim, dispute or controversy between the parties had to be arbitrated under the Federal Arbitration Act. The relationship between the distributors and Flowers soured and the distributors sued Flowers. Company said they couldn’t sue but had to arbitrate instead because of the Federal Arbitration Act and their contracts. Bissonnette and Wojnarowski responded that they did not have to arbitrate pointing to an exemption in the Act. That exemption said that the Act did not apply to seamen, railroad employees or any other class of workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce. Now, we had held years ago that the other class of workers covered by the exemption had to be transportation workers, just like the seamen and railroad employees were. Bissonnette and Wojnarowski said they fit the bill because they transported the baked goods from the warehouse to the stores. But the Court of Appeals whose decision we review today ruled that that was not enough to trigger the exemption. The workers it said also had to work in a transportation industry and it held that Bissonnette and Wojnarowski worked in the bakery business, not the transportation industry so they were not covered by the exemption and had to arbitrate. Today we reverse that decision. Whether the exemption applies depends on what the worker does, not what his or her employer does, so it doesn’t matter that Flowers is in the bakery business, what matters is whether Bissonnette and Wojnarowski are transportation workers. Think what would happen if the rule were different. Courts would have to try to figure out how to categorize businesses when that might be as easy as it might seem. Is something a transportation business or something else? For example, is a pizza delivery company in the delivery business or the pizza business? What about companies like Amazon or Walmart, they sell their own products but also deliver products from others. Are they transportation companies or something else? Our reading of the Federal Arbitration Act saves us from having to answer those riddles. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is vacated and the case is sent back for some further proceedings on other issues. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
